sourceDesc.p: Downloaded from https://medium.com/@louBurnard/deplacement-vers-la-vergne-3-8807d28c5353 and translated from HTML to TEI

div.head: Déplacement vers La Vergne, #3
div.p: Can you get from Grenoble to La Vergne by train? Just about, is the short answer, but you can’t do it in one day. Or not when I tried anyway. Here’s how it went…

figure.head: This of course is the view from my usual hotel in Grenoble. It’s late December so there’s an Xmas market in full swing.

div.p: On 17 December 2014, the morning after my small contribution to the political history of Digital Humanities (en France), I said goodbye to the Hotel Europe in sunny downtown Grenoble and set off for la gare, backpack on my back….

The first train of many is the 1022 departure TER 17616, over the hills and far away to Lyon PartDieu. I have done the journey before, but in the opposite direction, so I am anticipating lots of splendid mountain scenery and delightful prospects as the the train totters up to Le Tour du Pin and down again on the other side. On this occasion however, the weather is not favourable. Rain sets in and continues for the rest of the two hour journey.

At Lyon I just have time to pop into the Tall Voyagers Saloon for a drink and a pee, before catching the next train, TER 86610 to Roanne, wherever that is, via all sorts of implausibly named stations (see display)
The rain sets in with a vengeance, and the landscape becomes increasingly soggy, thwarting my natural desire to document the details of the picturesque countryside I am trundling through.
By the time we get to Roanne (13h30) I am seriously in need of lunch, and it is still far too wet. But what is this I spy, just opposite the station?

It’s a proper French eating establishment, the (slightly) cheaper kid brother of a very posh eaterie known as the Troisgros, (“three fatties” ?) for which Roanne is apparently renowned, though not hitherto by me. I enter and throw my unworthy self on the mercy of the charming serveuse. Une place pour dejeuner, sans reservation? We will see what the chef says. Fortunately, the chef is willing, presumably since he still has some of the set lunch left and the clientele is already well into dessert. The formule sur la pouce turns out to be a tasty vegetable tart, followed by what I suspect of being beef tongue, lightly poached and served with a piquant sauce, quite a gastronomic treat compared with what one normally finds in the cafe opposite the station. Thus fortified, I consider rainy Roanne, which seems to have little to recommend it, except maybe the distant prospect of one day being linked by TGV to somewhere else (it seems)

figure.head: Would it change your life to be 90 minutes from Paris? Well, depends on the weather I’d say.

div.p: There’s an enormous block of offices next door to the station, at the end of which I find two fat policemen contemplating a traffic junction and a very large puddle. Back to the station, where I sit on the deserted platform I, trying to avoid the rain and waiting for the 15h48 stopping train to Clermont-Ferrand, TER 75736

This chugs through the drizzle, stopping here and there, finally reaching my destination, Riom Chatel Guyon, with five minutes to spare before the TER 73058, coming from Clermont-Ferrand arrives. (Take a look on this map: you’ll realise that this is one of those very rare occasions where the French railway system actually seems to have been planned to make transversal journeys feasible)
This, my last train of the day, leaves on time at 1702, as the night starts to fall. It’s a Bombardier. It’s warm. It’s dry. Excellent.

And so via various implausibly named towns and villages (Pontmort, Aubiat, Aigueperse, Gannat, St Bonnet de Rochefort, and Commentry) we chunter across the Auvergne, cautiously skirting the massif central. Commentry was once, it appears, a coal mining town, and also has the distinction of being the first commune to elect a socialist mayor (in 1883) but today it just looks wet. Finally, at 1830 I arrive at the charming railway junction town of Montluçon, ready for a quick dinner and bed. The latter is provided by the Hotel Le Faisan, which I chose on the grounds that it seemed to be neither as impossibly luxurious as one of its neighbours nor as blandly corporate as the other; the former by a surprisingly pink establishment called Le Scott Resto just across the road from the hotel, of which I will say nothing. Given the robust nature of its owner’s responses to some critical reviews on TripAdvisor, you may attribute this to sheer cowardice on my part if you wish.

The next morning I am up early to do some sightseeing and the rain seems to have stopped. First up, the splendid memorial outside the station, commemorating the gallant resistance of the good citizens of Montluçon when during the Occupation some of them were forced to work the coal fields for the Boches.

Next, it’s a pleasant enough stroll down the broad Avenue Marx-Dormoy, which in normal times is dominated by this statue of the great man behind the 1920s war memorial. Today however, it is dominated by a rather tacky Christmas funfair, which somewhat encroaches on his space.

figure.head: Statue commemorating Marx-Dormoy apparently just getting out of bed: there’s a better picture here

div.p: The Avenue joins up with the central shopping street, down which I wander in search of toiletries and a nice view of the Modern Bar.

But the main architectural presence is of course the Château des ducs de Bourbon à Montluçon, which sits on top of the hill around which this and all the other streets cluster in approved medieval fashion.
All around the chateau, there are splendid viewpoints over the said streets, some of them with helpful if outdated indicator boards to show where various establishments used to be, back in the day:

figure.head: Syndicat d’Initiatives is a phrase you don’t hear much these days

div.p: Down below there are indeed narrow twisty little medieval streets full of picturesque half timbered houses, ancient monuments etc.

Definitely worth a revisit when the weather has improved. For the moment, however, it’s time to head back to the station for the final train of the trip, the very last (almost) part of my journey, which is the 11h34 TER 68703 to Gueret, a one hour journey via Huriel, Lavaufranche, Gouzon, Parsac, and Busseau-sur-Creuse etc. On the map it’s a very wiggly train line, and in the flesh (as it were) probably highly scenic. I say “probably” because the one-carriage train itself turned about to be so dirty after yesterday’s rain that I could scarcely see through the window of my notionally first class compartment.

figure.head: TER 68703 waiting to depart from Montlucon. In need of a good wash.

div.p: No matter. I arrived in Gueret unscathed, where Wim and Anke met me, loaded me into their car and drove me to Dun Le Palestel for our appointment with Maitre de Lisle. After which final transaction, I am now the proud owner of No. 3 La Vergne. Wim and Anke, unimpressed, drop me at the Intermarche supermarket and drive off into their own life. And so, the last leg of my journey to La Vergne was perforce accomplished by an over priced taxi.

Postscript: and that is how I came to spend my first night in La Vergne, brrr brr, on Dec 18, 2014. The following day, I got a lift to La Souterraine with Duane, and took the train to Limoges, where I had another day to wait before catching an aeroplane to Southampton, and thus back to Oxford. But that's another story.
